text: So, I'm going to talk about DIP PLC, which has a few sort of slightly surprising properties
to it. It's a slightly confusing system. What does it mean? A self-authenticating decentralized
identifier, which is strongly consistent, recoverable, and has key rotation. Okay,
so four things. Strongly consistent, recoverable, self-authenticating, and key rotation.
And the slightly weird thing about this is, it does have all these properties, but not
all at the same time. As far as I'm aware, it's technically impossible to have all these
properties at the same time. Let me explain what I mean. So, what do we mean when we say
self-authenticating? We mean that you can look at the data, and you can confirm it's valid
without looking at anything else. Okay, so the classic example is an IPFS document. If
you have the ID of an IPFS document, that is a hash. So, if somebody you don't trust
it gives you the document, you can then recreate that hash and make sure that it's correct.
Okay? So, how does this look in DIP PLC? Well, when you first create a new account in DIP PLC,
it's exactly like that, right? So, for your first account, you make a document. You put in,
for example, your blue sky handle, your PDS, a load of other stuff, and you put in the key
that's going to control this account. This is called the rotation key, and you take the hash of this
document, and that hashes your ID. Okay? So, this is exactly like IPFS, self-authenticating content
address. Then, if you want to make a change, what we're going to do is we're going to replace maybe
one of the, maybe replace the handle, replace one of the fields in there, and then we're going to sign
it with the key that we stated in that previous update. Okay? So, this is also cryptographically
verifiable. This is also basically self-authenticating, assuming you've got the whole chain.
If I want to check this, I can check the signature on this second one. I can then check that it matches
the key in the first one, and then I can check the hash of the first one. So, I can authenticate the
whole chain. I don't need to ask anybody else. Okay? But we also have this property of key rotation.
So, what happens when we want to rotate the key? So, the first thing we're going to do is
we're going to make a new document with our new key that we want to change, and we're going to sign
it with our old key. Okay? And then, once we've done that, we can now sign all our future updates with
the new key. And as far for normal key rotation, that means that it doesn't matter what happens to
the old key. Okay? We can leak that on the internet if we like. It doesn't matter. It's rotated out.
You do this because you no longer trust the old key. Okay? So, that's what we've done. The problem is,
what happens if somebody does this? If somebody takes the old key, and they put an update chained on
top of one of my early updates, and they pretend that these later updates didn't happen. Okay? So,
if you have this situation where somebody is in control of one of your old keys, this is no longer
self-authenticating. Okay? It's no longer self-authenticating because we did key rotation.
So, we've got these kind of really different properties. Even though we all have dids, and they
look the same, different dids have really fundamentally different security properties.
Some dids, your initial document is self-authenticating a content address. Okay?
The one that you then updated is also self-authenticating. Okay? Right down at the other end,
if you've rotated your key and the earlier key has leaked, then this is not at all self-authenticating.
Okay? And then in the middle, there's this situation that a lot of people who have migrated their
PDS are in, where you have the key in your history, but that key hasn't leaked. Right? So, if you're on
Blue Sky, you've probably got their rotation key in your history, but that key, as far as we know,
is still secure. So, there is only one chain of your identity, but we don't know what will happen in
future. So, this situation at the top is very, very blessed. Right? This is wonderful. This is just like,
this has the same security as, like, an IPFS hash or a Bitcoin or an Ethereum address or, for example,
a Nostra ID. So, if you run into, like, a Nostra enthusiast who is concerned about, like,
I heard about this central server that I'm going to talk to you about. You can tell them, if you are
happy with the Nostra feature set, right? Because the way they do it in Nostra is just don't do key
rotation. You just don't lose your key. If you're happy with the Nostra feature set, you can have
Nostra-style security with DIP PLC. Right? But once you use this feature set, in certain circumstances
that are kind of out of our control, we then lose this security property and we end up with kind of a
really differently secured system. So, right down at the bottom, we're not self-authenticating.
We've got Sadako crawling out of our TV screens. Our DID is cursed. We can no longer verify just by
looking at it whether it's correct or not. Okay? So, the solution in DIP PLC is to use a trusted
sequencer. So, the job of a sequencer is to say, this is when this update was published. And since I
know that this one was published earlier, I then know that the evil one that we did later is invalid.
Right? So, they can choose the canonical chain and the correct way to resolve your ID.
And there's another slightly confusing thing about the DIP PLC system, which is that this server
actually has two jobs. And they're really, really different jobs and have kind of really different
properties. So, it's a sequencer, but it's also a data publisher. And you didn't have to do these
together, right? These are fundamentally different things. They could have been... They're different
roles. They could have been done differently. And they have really different implications.
Okay. So, what happens if this trusted server goes rogue? And you might think, okay, why is it going to go
rogue? I'll give you one example of what I mean by rogue. So, we had this issue in Turkey
where there was a legal proceeding in Turkey that said that Blue Sky had to ban some Turkish dissidents.
Okay? And they didn't do this at the level of the directory. They did this just at the level of their
PDS. But what this shows us is these well-meaning people, they are not in the tank for the prime
minister of Turkey. Right? But well-meaning people can have pressure put on them to do things that they
would not necessarily want to do. Okay? Because they live in the real world. If they didn't do that,
they couldn't change planes in Istanbul. And potentially, for example, the Blue Sky app would
be banned from the App Store, at the very least in Turkey and potentially everywhere else. Okay?
So, there's now a plan which, until today, I thought was a good plan, to go with a separate
organization. And just thinking about my own talk, I realize it may not be a good plan. To go with a
separate organization that's going to run the directory, there's going to be a board of trustworthy
people on this, you know, on this board in control of it. And they're genuinely, you know,
as far as I can tell, trustworthy people. So, let's assume that we succeed in the task of keeping
psychopaths off this board, which is an unsolved problem in, like, human governance, right?
So, generally, you know, the first generation may not have psychopaths, but they seem to somehow get
there. But let's assume that they're all honest, well-meaning people, and they care about Proto.
Let's look at a similar example. Private universities in the United States have a kind of a similar
governance structure. And they're actually a really good case for this kind of governance structure.
So, if you're trying to govern a private university in the US, you've got a bunch of alumni who really
care about the institution, right? They really care about the school. And if they don't care about that,
they care about their own personal reputations. So, this is a really good case for this governance
that we're doing with PLC directory. But what happened was that the Trump administration put
pressure on them and took a bunch of things that they cared about hostage, right? So, they said,
"If you don't do the things that we want, that we don't normally have the rights to make you do,
we are going to cancel grants that your researchers are due. We're going to cancel the visas of
overseas students at your university." So, these people were put in this dilemma where they can
have the policies that they think they can have, or they can compromise. And if they compromise,
then they're going to end up hurting far fewer people than if they do what they're being told to do.
So, ultimately, what, for example, Columbia and a lot of other universities did was they threw trans
people and Palestine protesters under the bus to protect the majority of their institution, okay?
So, if you think about this with AppProto, if you care about AppProto, there are all kinds of ways that
people can put pressure on you, right? Can you get AppProto apps into the App Store? Are you going to have
network level blocks of the PLC directory? All kinds of things that you could do that will potentially
put a dilemma on the people who are governing this structure, even if they're well-meaning people,
and cause them to do something that we may not think is what the social contract would tell them to do,
okay? So, let's assume they go rogue. Can we just do things? Because I've got my client,
right? It's free software. We've got our relays. We know anybody can run the relays. We've got our own PDSs.
There's nothing that says that we have to look at the PLC directory. We can do what we like.
So, if I've got a self-authentic did, this is probably actually okay.
So, here's what we can do. Somebody can set up the too hot for PLC directory.
The records that are being messed with by the PLC directory, what we can do is we can query the PLC directory.
We can look at our own data, and if the PLC directory refuses to answer for that record or if it's done
some weird -- well, no, if it's refusing to answer or if it's not giving us the whole chain of updates
that we've got, then we can just serve what we've got, okay? And the great thing about this is we don't
really need consensus to do this, right? We can have, like, bitterly, mutually detesting factions of
that proto, and you can make your directory and I can make my directory, but since this is self-authentic
data we're looking at, we're ultimately all going to get to the same result, right? As long as we share
the data, we can all get to the same result. So, we have this property that we wanted. I don't know if we
still have strong consistency. I think we do. The problem is, okay, what about the sequencing part?
Because the sequencing part is something where only the directory is allowed to make a decision,
right? And so, the sequencing -- it's making a decision about what time did I receive this update?
And somebody else may honestly get that update at a different time. So, sequencing requires us to just
have this single directory, and that's why it's designed like this, okay? So, we can do things,
but we all need to move together. We need to do, like, a flag day. We need to all say, like, from this
day onwards, we're using somebody else for the directory. So, can we do that? Maybe we can.
There are some sort of favorable precedents. So, this happens in free software all the time, right?
So, Oracle took over OpenOffice and did all kinds of nasty Oracle-type things. And the developers and
the users of OpenOffice just kind of all got together and decided to use a different project,
different GitHub repo, different brand. And everybody moved to LibreOffice, and basically,
nobody uses OpenOffice anymore. Same thing with some blockchain systems. So, Steam was one where
there was this thing where the developers of a protocol, for some reason that I don't quite understand,
had particular rights to do things to the protocol. And Steam got bought up by Justin Sun, who is this
kind of crypto-comedy villain. And the community, both the developers and the user, didn't want this,
so they forked the project to Hive. They said, from this point in the history, like, from like 10 o'clock
tomorrow, we're going to use our own version of the software. We're going to take out all the special
privileges that Justin Sun would have had, and we're going to carry on. And they did that, and it worked.
And it's often been done, and it's often worked. So, what have we got to do to replace a malfunctioning
PLC directory? We all have to recognize the problem, okay? We have to agree a new person who's going to run it,
and we have to get a snapshot of the directory from some point in time, when we're going to switch
over and start using the new one. So, the first problem is, do we agree that it broke, right?
So, ideally, the rogue directory would say, we're going rogue, right? We're going to screw you all
over. This is now the wrong picture. This should be-- it's Filippo here. This should be Filippo. Anyway,
ideally, they will actually announce that they're going to screw us over. But the problem is,
they're the adversary, right? They're not trying to be helpful to us. And the adversary gets to pick
the battle, right? So, if their goal is, let's say that they've decided there are a bunch of bad actors
actors on AppRoto. And they need to get these bad actors out of the directory so that they can get
AppRoto's spreading properly in corporate environments, which are stubbornly resistant,
whatever they want to do, right? They get to set the precedents, so they can reorg out Jesse Single.
I don't know if you're familiar with this guy. There's a guy who's really, really unpopular on
Blue Sky. He writes nasty things about trans people. Blue Sky users are always badgering Blue Sky to ban
this person. And Blue Sky is saying, well, he hasn't actually violated the terms of service, so we can't.
If you do something that, to me, looks rogue but is reorg out Jesse Single, this is very popular among
Blue Sky users. Another thing they can do is they can pick at the factional fault lines. So, right now,
we're at this very blessed time of a new decentralized community where basically everybody's friends.
And you can just see here and there the little green shoots of what in the future will be a vast forest
of bitter factional grudges. And they can work on these, right? You know, they can do something to
somebody who's a bad guy to one of the factions and the other faction loves us. Because what they've got to
do here is they've got to prevent us from forming consensus. And it's much, much harder to form a
consensus than it is to disrupt the consensus. Forming consensus is really hard. So, if we're trying to
form a consensus about we're going to move over to this guy because of this problem, they get to pick
at those fault lines and make it hard for us. Okay, next problem is, do we have an up-to-date mirror?
So, right now, there's loads of really good work going on in Blue Sky and outside Blue Sky to make it
really easy to run a mirror of the PLC directory. So, ideally, what happens here is, you know, loads and
loads of people have got their own mirrors. People aren't really relying too much on the PLC directory.
But in any case, we can always get the latest data and we can say, from this point in time,
we're not using the old directory, we're using the new directory. Okay?
The problem is that they're the adversary, right? They're not trying to help us. And our ability to
get this mirror is dependent on the adversary. So, if you look at what any organization does before it
screws the users over, the number one thing, day zero of initialification, is to dismantle the
accountability mechanisms, right? Dismantle anything that will give your users the ability to exit.
So, for example, if you're Instagram or Reddit or Twitter, then you're going to turn off your API.
This happens before the actual, like, the algorithms go to shit and your application experience becomes
useless. First, you turn off the APIs and then you wait a bit. And that way, nobody else has got a way
to help you exit, to give you an alternative. Another example. Week one of the Trump administration,
on the first Friday night, he fired the 17th inspector general, who were supposed to be the
independent observers who would hold him accountable for corruption in his administration. If you're
actually going to do corruption, you remove the things that will hold you accountable for corruption.
Okay? So, what would it look like? Well, the mirrors just sort of stopped working on a Friday night,
you know? Nobody seems to be able to pull from them anymore, don't know what's going on. I'm hitting
this end point. It's not working. It's working for me. It's not working for you. You know, you get
somebody to answer on a Tuesday and they'll say, "Oh, we're looking into it. You know, maybe waste our
time a little bit. Give us, like, a URL for some log files so they see if you can help, you know,
see if you can diagnose the problem." And it's like eight terabytes of just irrelevant log files,
you know? They've got all the cards here. Another thing they can do is they can spam the mirrors to
death because one of the amazing things about this system is that it's designed to kind of hold
infinity records. Like, there's no cost to writing to it. The only limitation on putting loads of stuff
in the PLC directory is that the operator is doing some rate limiting on, like, IPs and PDSs and stuff
like that. But in theory, as far as I know, everything is supposed to go in there. So,
they can allow other people spam in there. They can turn off the rate limiting until the mirrors fall
over. They can also create their own spam. And the great thing about creating your own spam is you can
do this deterministically from a secret seed. The mirrors have to hold all the data to have a complete
set. You don't even have to hold the data. All you need is your seed and a serial number,
and you can serve it. So, they've got all the cards when it comes to, like, spamming the mirrors and
just making it impossible for people to hold them accountable. Okay. So, what can we do to mitigate
this? I should be clear these aren't quite solutions, but they will help. So, we can run lots of mirrors,
right? And this is something that's already, like, already happening a little bit.
So, we can – when we write, instead of writing to the PLC directory, we can write to the mirror
and have the mirror keep a log and share what they write, and then forward that on to the directory.
So, that way, if the directory isn't giving us all its data, we should still kind of maybe be able to piece
some of it together. Try to get consensus on what the mirrors are actually showing us.
So, if the mirroring stops working on a Friday, we want also – we at least want to know that as of Thursday,
we all know what it should be showing. There's then some technical things that I'll come to.
This is really important. We need to figure out what is the social contract to did PLC and get this
really clear and get this clear as soon as we possibly can. Like, I read the spec with my crypto brain,
and it talks about avoiding censorship. And to my – like, in crypto, like, if you're preventing a
ransomware payment from going to Kim Jong-un, that's censorship. But, like, I don't know what the rest of
the app proto community thinks about this. Like, is it the job of the PLC directory to accommodate
everybody, you know, spammers, scammers, malware authors? Are they all supposed to go in there or
not? Like, what's the deal? What happens to, you know, child porn or accounts linking to child porn?
What is the actual job of this directory? What is it that would tell us if it was going rogue?
I don't know if that's clear to some people. It's not clear to me. So – and the later we leave
this, the harder it is to figure it out, not least because we'll have all the factional squabbling.
So some more things – some practical things you can do. Keep your ID self-authenticating, right?
So remember I said earlier we had the blessed dids where you could fully validate just from
available data, and we had the cursed dids with Sadako crawling out of your TV screen,
where we potentially had a fork in the timeline, or we had, like, potentially somebody could create a
data fork in the timeline. So if you keep your own rotation key, if, when you sign up, you make your
own – make your own rotation key, write down the seed phrase or whatever you do, and don't lose that,
you probably never need to change it, right? I mean, you know, Nostra doesn't do rotation keys,
it seems like a – there's an obvious pattern if you're using most out-processed stuff where you feel
like you register on Blue Sky first, and then you move to whatever PDS you were hoping to stick
with. It's better if you can just go straight to the PDS, and that way you don't have this rotation
key in your history that is controlled by Blue Sky. I don't quite – okay, I put don't use it.
This is a slightly too strong a case, but there's this – remember we had recoverable in the four
features. So there's this feature in DIP PLC where if you have a high-profile, like a high-priority
rotation key and a low-priority rotation key, you can cancel the actions of the low
priority one with the high priority one. And this is sort of useful, but it automatically
creates this fork in your dead, right? It automatically causes Sadako to come crawling
out of your TV screen and reduces your – the security properties of your account.
So, one more thing. If you are hosting PDSs for somebody else, a dead rotation key is toxic waste,
right? Plan the life cycle of your rotation keys. If you use – I don't know if this is still true,
but when I tried it – if you use the stock PDS that Blue Sky provided, it will set one rotation key for
all your users. And that means that if one of your users moves off your PDS, then you still can't delete
it because you need it for other users. It would be better to do what we do with the signing keys and create it
when they sign up and then delete it if they move off, okay? So, all this just makes it – it makes the
individual user safer, but it also means that in the case where we have to do this switch from a rogue PLC
directory, we have far less ambiguous data that we have to somehow deal with.
So, okay. Let me talk a bit about ways that this can be more robust. And the first one I'm very
pleased to say is happening. This is really cool. So, T-logs. We heard from Filippo earlier about T-logs.
This should make it much more reliable to mirror. We can all tell that we've got the same mirror quite
easily. We can quite easily sync up on the same thing. And we've got like a log of the history of
what the sequence actually sequenced. So, this is really important. We can have loads of people with
like very lightweight devices verifying that that log is being appended to and not like reorg, as I
understand it. And we can also have people run monitors who check that the rules are actually
correctly applied. So, this is already great and it will really help. Ideally, we will push this as far
down the stack as we possibly can. So, ideally, if the directory stops cooperating with the system,
we want stuff to break. But this is the problem of having this kind of single position of authority.
They've got the official organization. They've got the single right privileges. The problem with this is
they have the ability to just kind of turn that stuff off, right? Because if you're a developer,
you know, you've got a choice between no records and invalid records. What are you going to do,
right? Unless we can really, really quickly coordinate this flag and move to a new directory. And this is the
difference with how certificate transparency works with certificate authorities, right? With like
certificate transparency for SSL certificates. Because there, if you have a bad certificate authority that
refuses to participate in the or breaks its participation in the scheme, which apparently
does sometimes happen, your users can always get a certificate from another authority. But this is
specifically the power that we don't have, at least in protocol in this system. You cannot move sequencer
in didplc. All we can do is try to coordinate this incredible flag day. There's a proposal a while back
by Nick, who's someone you know, to make it possible to choose your directory. I don't think this allows
you to escape from a bad directory, but I think it potentially really helps. So that instead of having
this single privileged position of the didplc directory that is in charge of didplc,
you potentially have a bunch of different operators. So I really like this. And then,
finally, I'm going to talk about some decentralized consensus approaches. So there are two
serious problems with decentralized consensus for this application and for a lot of approach applications.
The first is that, like, in the American culture wars, everything must have a side. And apparently,
like, an open validator set is MAGA-coded nowadays, right? So, like, you can't say blockchain. You know,
you have to kind of reinvent blockchain but not tell people. So there's that problem. And then the other
problem is writing to a decentralized ledger generally costs you money. And the reason why it costs you money is
because it needs to somehow manage spam, right? So if you don't want to put anybody in charge of
deciding what is a legitimate transaction, and also if you want to preserve the ability to
mirror the entire dataset, then the only way I think that anybody knows is to charge money to write.
So a couple of approaches. Well, there's a BFT system that somebody wrote. I think a lot of this stuff
could probably be rewritten in the light of T-logs. But there's a BFT suggestion using Comet BFT.
I haven't looked very hard at it, but it's maybe interesting. A couple of ideas by me. The idea here
is if we're going to use a blockchain, we want to have to avoid writing to it all the time. And we
especially want to avoid regular users having to write to it. So a couple of things. P2P guardrails.
This is a suggestion where, firstly, you can change your sequencer. So if the directory is bad,
you can escape. Secondly, if the sequencer refuses to sequence something for you, then you or somebody
on your behalf can send it to the blockchain and we can read it off there. And finally, we do some
reorg protection based on kind of T-log-like checksums so that you can prove that you previously
had something already accepted and that it shouldn't be reorg'd. And then finally,
this one, didcow. This is a proposal for a new kind of did. And actually, what set me thinking about
this was I saw that people are trying to do organizational control of dids. So, for example,
you've got a decentralized organization, you've got a bunch of different people, and they all want to
share control in some way of a did. And this is something that some of the blockchain tooling does
really, really well. So I thought it would be useful if you had kind of an explicitly kind of blockchain
oriented way of doing this. And the proposal is very simply, you make an ID with the controller blockchain
address that wraps your regular ID. You don't have to do a blockchain transaction to write this
because if there's nothing on the blockchain, we're just going to use the thing that was in your address.
And then if you want to move your wrapped ID, let's say you lose trust in did PLC, or you want to move
from a cursed PLC entry to a non-cursed one, or you want to move to a did web or a different did web,
whatever you want to do, you can repoint this one with a blockchain transaction.
Okay, so that's all I got. I've put links for all the technical things I talked about up here,
and I've also pinned this to my profile on GoatNavy on Blue Sky.
That clock says one minute, but we actually have a couple of minutes. So do we have any questions?
And actually, no, first, thank you. This was amazing.
Okay. So why don't we just all use did webs and then push the fight to the DNS layer?
Yeah. If you'd asked me that a year ago, I would have said we should all use did webs. But at that
time, I had very naive assumptions about the nation state governed liberal institutions
like, with a did web, everything is under a registrar, which is under a nation state. And
it seems like nation states will turn to fascism even within like a three year time span or something.
So I don't feel like that confident, like having a did web for the next 20 years. If it was five
years, I'm kind of cool with the did web. So yeah. So I think for long term, there's that. And then
the practical problem with did web is just that people have a hard time not losing their control of
their own of their domain. And also that the registrar will keep putting the price up with the domain as well.
Hi. Thank you for all of the list of things we can do to screw everybody over.
No. Okay. Seriously. Speaking of my personal capacity. But okay. So homework. Sounds like we should
make the policies for what is and isn't going to go in explicit so they will bind us for the future.
Yes.
We should like Tlogs are good. I saw here. And maybe considered Tlog witnesses, Tlog mirrors, Tlog monitors.
Well monitors are permissionless. But witnesses. Anything else?
Right. So apart from kind of changing the whole system. Actually, one thing that occurred to me the
other day was it would make more sense to separate out the reading and the sequencing. Right? So if you are
writing that Tlog, you don't necessarily need to be running the directory. Does that make sense? Right?
You need your own version of the directory and you give proofs to people when they asked to write to it.
But anybody could be running those directories. And I think it would be really useful if we could just
avoid this social point of like the PLC directory, which is clearly like the dictator of all things PLC.
That would really help. So like at least potentially, you know, maybe have different organizations.
Maybe Blue Sky should keep PLC directory and you should just do sequencing at the non-profit.
And the non-sequencing part here is just a read-only service that makes things available. Is that right?
Well, actually, I mean, there's a couple of ways to do it. One way to do it would be just to do timestamps.
So you would literally get a message and say, "What time did I give you this message?" And then you give it back to
them with a signature. But I think you could do something similar with the T log, like some feature of T logs.
Yeah, I think the T log equivalent is a read-only replica, which can also choose to the set of
witnesses that signed that T log. But that can be done permissionlessly. Like anybody can spin one
of those up downstream. Anybody can spring up a mirror. But what I want to communicate here is that the
switching over from the PLC, like the PLC, it's a social arrangement, just as much as a technical
arrangement, right? So who do people think runs the PLC? And this is why earlier I said, like, until I was
practicing this talk, I thought it was a good idea to move to a new organization. And now I don't, because
the new organization will actually have much more authority than the old organization. Like, if I look at
those really good social recovery operations, they're all against some evil corporation. So we can
we can totally paint blue sky as an evil corporation, and like organize this social recovery to foil their
evil plans. But like with a Swiss organization that's, you know, it's...
Worse reputation is that better. You have to wear, you have to wear hoods with the robes with hoods,
basically. And then yeah, okay. Let's take two more. Anybody else?
Well, this is maybe going into the weeds a bit too much. But I'm curious why the did cow proposal
didn't have included chain ID, if I understand correctly, the address is a smart contract.
That's correct. The reason why it didn't include a chain ID was big was two reasons. Firstly, it's already
quite long. And secondly, the big burden, I think actually, of those two proposals,
and also probably the reason why the guardrails proposal is actually obviously dead in the water,
is that you need a, you need an RPC endpoint, right? You need a connection to the block,
a reconnection to the blockchain to resolve anything. So if we have lots of other chains there,
then whoever is resolving this has got to have lots of different working RPC endpoints. So I'm
kind of like, okay, what's the simplest thing that could work? You know, one RPC, no chain ID.
